title: Ageratina saltillensis
intro: Ageratina saltillensis, also known as Mexican mint or Mexican ageratina, is a perennial shrub native to Mexico and Central America, belonging to the mint family (Lamiaceae). The plant has been traditionally used in folk medicine to treat various health issues, including fever, cough, and digestive problems, due to its anti-inflammatory, antiseptic, and antiviral properties, which are beneficial for immune system support and wound healing. The bioactive constituents of Ageratina saltillensis include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and sesquiterpenes, which contribute to its medicinal properties. The most common herbal preparations made from this plant include teas, infusions, and tinctures, which are often used to alleviate respiratory issues, such as bronchitis and asthma.
taxonomy: Ageratina saltillensis is classified within the plant family Asteraceae. This plant is a member of the genus Ageratina, which is a group of flowering plants commonly known as mountain silver or ageratina. Ageratina saltillensis is native to Mexico and is often found in mountainous regions. It belongs to the order Asterales and the clade Rosidae. The classification of Ageratina saltillensis is still a subject of ongoing research and may be subject to revisions.

properties: Ageratina saltillensis, also known as False Dragon Head, has been studied for its potential therapeutic properties. Its extracts have been found to possess antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and antimicrobial activities, which may contribute to its traditional uses.
